drivability: Usually unsafe to drive for long periods; short-distance driving only if absolutely necessary
possible_causes_ranked: Fuel delivery issue such as low pressure or contaminated fuel
Low compression or mechanical engine issue
diagnostic_checks: Scan freeze-frame and live misfire counters
Inspect spark plugs for wear, gap, oil fouling, or carbon buildup
Check ignition coil output and coil boot condition
Inspect intake system and vacuum hoses for leaks
Verify fuel pressure and fuel quality
Check injector balance if one or more cylinders appear weaker
Perform compression or leak-down test if ignition/fuel checks pass
risk_if_ignored: Engine damage if caused by a mechanical fault
common_misdiagnoses: Replacing the catalytic converter before fixing the misfire
Replacing all coils without identifying the actual failed cylinder or root cause
Ignoring vacuum leaks or low fuel pressure
customer_explanation: Your engine is misfiring on multiple cylinders. This can cause shaking, poor power, and possible catalytic converter damage if driven too long.
mechanic_explanation: Multiple-cylinder misfire detected. Prioritize ignition inspection, fuel delivery verification, intake leak testing, and mechanical integrity if the misfire pattern is widespread.
technician_notes: If the check engine light is flashing, catalytic converter damage can occur quickly. Always rule out basic tune-up issues before deeper engine work.

solutions: Swap coil with cylinder 2 to test
recommended_priority: Repair soon; immediate if flashing MIL or severe shaking
drivability: May be drivable briefly, but performance and emissions are affected
possible_causes_ranked: Wiring or connector issue affecting coil or injector
diagnostic_checks: Swap cylinder 1 coil with another cylinder and see if misfire follows
Inspect and compare cylinder 1 spark plug to others
Listen for injector operation or perform injector balance test
Check cylinder 1 compression
Inspect connector pins and harness for coil/injector
common_misdiagnoses: Replacing the injector before testing the ignition coil
Replacing all spark plugs without checking compression
Ignoring harness or connector problems
customer_explanation: Cylinder 1 is not firing correctly. This can make the engine shake, lose power, and run poorly.
mechanic_explanation: Single-cylinder misfire on cylinder 1. Verify spark, injector pulse, coil function, and mechanical compression before replacing parts.
technician_notes: A quick coil swap test is often the fastest way to confirm a single-cylinder ignition fault.

drivability: Often drivable, but hesitation and poor performance are common
possible_causes_ranked: Vacuum leak on bank 1 or intake system
Weak fuel pump or restricted fuel filter
Unmetered air leak after the MAF
Exhaust leak ahead of upstream O2 sensor
diagnostic_checks: Review short-term and long-term fuel trims
Inspect vacuum hoses and intake ducting for cracks
Clean and test MAF sensor
Check fuel pressure and volume
Inspect PCV system for stuck-open condition
Check for exhaust leaks near front O2 sensor
risk_if_ignored: Overheating exhaust valves in severe cases
common_misdiagnoses: Replacing the O2 sensor when the real issue is a vacuum leak
Ignoring dirty or inaccurate MAF readings
Skipping fuel pressure testing
customer_explanation: The engine is running with too much air or not enough fuel on one side of the engine. This may cause hesitation, weak acceleration, or rough running.
mechanic_explanation: Lean condition detected on bank 1. Fuel trim analysis, intake leak testing, MAF validation, and fuel delivery checks should be prioritized.
technician_notes: Fuel trim data is the key clue. High positive trims at idle often point to a vacuum leak; high trims at load may suggest fuel delivery weakness.

recommended_priority: Repair after confirming root cause; do not replace catalyst blindly
drivability: Usually drivable, but emissions failure is likely and performance may degrade
possible_causes_ranked: Upstream or downstream O2 sensor reading issue
Exhaust leak before or near converter
Long-term misfire or rich/lean running that damaged the catalyst
Oil or coolant contamination in exhaust
likely_affected_parts: Ignition and fuel system if misfire or lean condition exists
diagnostic_checks: Check for current or history misfire codes
Inspect live data for upstream and downstream O2 sensor switching
Check for exhaust leaks ahead of or near the catalyst
Verify engine is not running rich, lean, or misfiring
Measure temperature or backpressure if catalyst restriction is suspected
risk_if_ignored: Potential catalyst clogging and power loss
common_misdiagnoses: Replacing the catalytic converter before fixing a misfire or fuel trim problem
Replacing O2 sensors without checking exhaust leaks
Ignoring oil consumption or coolant contamination
customer_explanation: The emissions system thinks the catalytic converter on bank 1 is not cleaning exhaust gases as well as it should.
mechanic_explanation: Catalyst efficiency monitor failed on bank 1. Confirm sensor integrity and upstream engine performance before condemning the converter.
technician_notes: P0420 is often a result code, not the root cause. If misfires or fuel trim issues are present, repair those first.

possible_causes_ranked: Contamination from oil, coolant, or fuel additives
diagnostic_checks: Monitor O2 sensor switching speed in live data
Inspect heater circuit operation
Inspect connector and harness condition
Check for exhaust leaks before sensor
common_misdiagnoses: Replacing the sensor without checking the heater circuit
Ignoring exhaust leaks near the sensor
Confusing slow response with a rich or lean condition
customer_explanation: The front oxygen sensor on bank 1 is responding too slowly. This can reduce fuel economy and may affect emissions.
mechanic_explanation: Slow upstream O2 response on bank 1. Verify heater operation, signal activity, wiring integrity, and exhaust sealing before replacement.
technician_notes: A lazy upstream sensor can distort fuel control and may contribute to lean/rich complaints.

description: Mass Air Flow Sensor Range/Performance Problem
possible_causes_ranked: Dirty or contaminated MAF sensor element
Vacuum leak or intake air leak
diagnostic_checks: Inspect air filter and intake tract
Check live airflow readings at idle and under load
Inspect intake duct between MAF and throttle body for cracks
Clean MAF sensor using proper MAF cleaner
Check power, ground, and signal integrity
risk_if_ignored: Potential catalyst damage if fuel control becomes unstable
common_misdiagnoses: Replacing the MAF sensor when the intake boot is cracked
Ignoring aftermarket intake fitment issues
Using the wrong cleaner on the MAF element
customer_explanation: The airflow sensor reading does not look correct. This can lead to stalling, poor fuel economy, and weak performance.
mechanic_explanation: MAF range/performance fault. Validate airflow readings against expected load and RPM, and inspect for intake leaks or contamination before sensor replacement.
technician_notes: Do not use carb or brake cleaner on the MAF element. Use sensor-safe MAF cleaner only.

description: Mass or Volume Air Flow Circuit Low Input
solutions: Verify power and ground to sensor
drivability: Can cause stalling or hard starting; repair promptly
possible_causes_ranked: Low reference voltage or poor ground
Blown fuse or ECU power issue
diagnostic_checks: Check MAF connector fitment and pin condition
Verify sensor power and ground
Inspect fuse and related power circuit
Monitor MAF signal value against expected airflow
Inspect air intake for disconnection or collapse
common_misdiagnoses: Replacing the sensor without checking power or ground
Ignoring a blown fuse or damaged harness
Confusing a large intake leak with a bad sensor
customer_explanation: The airflow sensor signal is too low. This can cause rough starting, poor idle, and stalling.
mechanic_explanation: Low-input MAF circuit fault. Prioritize electrical integrity checks before replacing the sensor.
technician_notes: A low-input code often points to an electrical problem rather than only a dirty sensor.

recommended_priority: Diagnose soon, especially if transmission shift quality is affected
drivability: Vehicle may still move normally, but shifting and speed-dependent systems may behave erratically
possible_causes_ranked: Cluster or control module interpretation fault
likely_affected_parts: ABS wheel speed sensor inputs on some platforms
diagnostic_checks: Compare scan tool vehicle speed to cluster reading
Inspect sensor wiring and connector condition
Check sensor signal while spinning driven wheels safely
Inspect tone ring or reluctor if accessible
Scan ABS and transmission modules for related faults
common_misdiagnoses: Replacing the speed sensor when the issue is ABS-related
Ignoring damaged tone rings or wheel bearing encoder faults
Assuming the cluster is bad before checking scan data
customer_explanation: The system is not receiving a reliable vehicle speed signal. This may affect the speedometer, shifting, ABS, or cruise control.
mechanic_explanation: VSS fault present. Compare module-reported speed signals and verify whether the issue originates from the transmission sensor, ABS input, or cluster interpretation.
technician_notes: On some vehicles, a wheel speed sensor fault can indirectly influence speed-related systems.

description: Intake Air Temperature Circuit High Input
possible_causes_ranked: Open circuit in IAT sensor wiring
diagnostic_checks: Inspect IAT connector and harness
Compare IAT reading to ambient temperature on cold start
Measure sensor resistance against temperature
Check reference voltage and signal return
common_misdiagnoses: Replacing the ECU too early
Ignoring open-circuit wiring or connector issues
Confusing sensor high-input with actual hot intake air
customer_explanation: The intake air temperature signal is reading too high electrically, which often means a sensor or wiring issue rather than actual hot air.
mechanic_explanation: IAT high-input fault. Check for open-circuit conditions and compare PID to ambient before replacing components.
technician_notes: A high-input IAT code usually means the ECM sees an unrealistically cold reading due to an open circuit.

description: Evaporative Emission (EVAP) System Leak Detected (Small Leak)
recommended_priority: Repair when convenient, but before emissions inspection
possible_causes_ranked: Loose, missing, or damaged gas cap
Leaking charcoal canister or vent line
Purge or vent valve sealing issue
diagnostic_checks: Inspect gas cap seal and cap tightness
Visually inspect EVAP hoses and canister lines
Perform EVAP smoke test
Command purge and vent valves with scan tool if supported
risk_if_ignored: Persistent check engine light masking new problems
common_misdiagnoses: Replacing the canister before checking the gas cap
Ignoring tiny hose cracks
Skipping smoke testing
customer_explanation: The fuel vapor system has a very small leak. This usually does not affect how the car drives, but it can cause a check engine light and emissions test failure.
mechanic_explanation: Small EVAP leak detected. Begin with gas cap inspection, then confirm with smoke testing and purge/vent functional checks.
technician_notes: Gas cap issues are common, but smoke testing is the best way to confirm a true small leak.

description: Left Front Wheel Speed Sensor Circuit Malfunction
recommended_priority: Repair soon for safety system restoration
drivability: Vehicle can usually be driven, but ABS and traction control may be reduced or disabled
diagnostic_checks: Inspect LF sensor connector and harness for flex damage
Check live wheel speed data while driving or spinning wheel
Inspect tone ring or encoder for cracks, rust, or debris
Measure sensor resistance or waveform depending on sensor type
risk_if_ignored: Longer stopping distance on slippery surfaces
common_misdiagnoses: Replacing the sensor when the tone ring is damaged
Ignoring wheel bearing encoder failure
Overlooking harness damage near suspension movement points
customer_explanation: The left front wheel speed sensor signal is not working correctly. This can turn on the ABS or traction control lights.
mechanic_explanation: LF wheel speed sensor circuit fault. Compare wheel speed data and inspect sensor, encoder, and harness integrity at the knuckle.
technician_notes: Wheel bearing play can sometimes damage the encoder or alter sensor gap.

drivability: Usually drivable, but fuel level information may be unreliable
possible_causes_ranked: Open circuit in fuel sender wiring
Failed fuel level sender inside tank
Poor connector contact at pump module
diagnostic_checks: Inspect pump module connector
Check sender resistance sweep
Verify continuity from sender to cluster or module
Compare scan data to cluster reading if available
common_misdiagnoses: Replacing the cluster before testing the sender
Ignoring corrosion at the pump connector
Assuming the full pump assembly is required without verifying sender-only serviceability
customer_explanation: The fuel level sender circuit appears open, so the fuel gauge may read incorrectly or stay on empty.
mechanic_explanation: Fuel sender open-circuit fault. Verify sender resistance path through the tank module before replacing the cluster.
technician_notes: If the sender is integrated into the pump module, replacing the full assembly is often the practical fix.

recommended_priority: Stop operation immediately and inspect before restart
possible_causes_ranked: Faulty oil pressure switch or sender
Clogged oil filter or restricted oil pickup
diagnostic_checks: Check oil level immediately
Inspect for external oil leaks
Replace or inspect oil filter if condition is unknown
Verify pressure with a mechanical gauge
Inspect sensor wiring and connector
Do not continue operation if true low pressure is confirmed
related_codes: Other engine protection or shutdown alerts
common_misdiagnoses: Replacing the sensor without confirming actual pressure
Ignoring clogged pickup or internal bearing wear
Continuing to operate after a shutdown warning
customer_explanation: The machine has detected low engine oil pressure. Stop operation immediately to avoid severe engine damage.
mechanic_explanation: Low oil pressure protection event. Confirm actual pressure mechanically before condemning the sender, and inspect lubrication system integrity immediately.
technician_notes: For heavy equipment, treat true low oil pressure as an engine protection event, not just a warning.

recommended_priority: Stop operation and allow controlled cool-down
drivability: Unsafe to continue operating under load
possible_causes_ranked: Water pump or fan performance issue
diagnostic_checks: Shut down safely and check coolant after cooling
Inspect radiator core for blockage
Check fan belt tension and condition
Verify coolant circulation and thermostat operation
Inspect for external leaks
common_misdiagnoses: Replacing the thermostat without checking airflow blockage
Ignoring low coolant or air pockets
Overlooking belt slip or fan drive problems
customer_explanation: The engine coolant temperature is too high. Stop operating the machine and let it cool before checking the cooling system.
mechanic_explanation: High coolant temperature protection event. Confirm coolant level, radiator airflow, thermostat operation, and fan drive integrity.
technician_notes: Never open a hot cooling system under pressure. Cooling package contamination is common on off-road equipment.

drivability: Operation may be intermittent or limited depending on affected controller
possible_causes_ranked: Low system voltage or voltage spikes
diagnostic_checks: Check battery voltage and charging voltage
Inspect main grounds and power distribution
Inspect controller connectors for moisture or corrosion
Perform harness wiggle test where faults are intermittent
Check whether other subsystem codes are also present
related_codes: Other controller communication or supply voltage faults
risk_if_ignored: Potential controller damage from unstable voltage
common_misdiagnoses: Replacing the controller before checking power and grounds
Ignoring corrosion in connectors
Overlooking battery or charging system instability
customer_explanation: The machine has detected an electronic control system fault. This may cause intermittent operation, display issues, or shutdown behavior.
mechanic_explanation: Electronic control system fault. Validate system voltage quality, ground integrity, and harness condition before replacing controllers.
technician_notes: Electronic control faults are often caused by poor power or grounding, not the controller itself.
